skills.name: TypeScript
skills.why: TypeScript is the default language for every full-stack role. If you can't write typed code fluently you lose interviews.

topics: Types: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, void, any, unknown, never — know when to use each
Arrays & objects: typed arrays (string[]), typed objects, optional fields with ?
Functions: parameter types, return types, optional params, default params, rest params
Type aliases vs interfaces: when to use type vs interface — interfaces are extendable, types are composable
Union types: string | number — handle each case with type guards (typeof, instanceof)
Intersection types: TypeA & TypeB — combine types
Enums: const enums vs regular enums, string enums
Type assertions: as keyword, when to use and when to avoid
tsconfig.json: strict mode, target, module, paths — understand every option you set
Generics: function generics , class generics, constrained generics
Utility types: Partial, Required, Readonly, Pick, Omit, Exclude, Extract, ReturnType, Parameters
Mapped types: { [K in keyof T]: ... } — build types from other types
Conditional types: T extends U ? X : Y — type-level if statements
Template literal types: `${string}Id` — typed string patterns
Discriminated unions: { kind: 'a', ... } | { kind: 'b', ... } — the most common pattern in React
TypeScript with React: React.FC vs function signatures, event types, ref types, children prop
Type narrowing: if checks, in operator, instanceof, type predicates (x is Type)
Declaration merging, module augmentation
Infer keyword: extract types from complex types dynamically
Recursive types: tree nodes, JSON types, deeply nested structures
Variance: covariance vs contravariance in function types
Declaration files (.d.ts): writing types for untyped libraries
Project references: monorepo TypeScript setup
TypeScript compiler API: writing custom transformers (advanced)

resources.name: Total TypeScript Free Tutorials
resources.notes: Matt Pocock — the best TypeScript educator. Free exercises with in-browser TypeScript playground.

resources.name: TypeScript Official Handbook
resources.notes: Complete free reference written by the TypeScript team at Microsoft.

resources.name: TypeScript Playground
resources.notes: Free browser tool — run TypeScript instantly, see compiled JS, share snippets.

resources.name: JavaScript.info
resources.notes: Best free JavaScript fundamentals resource. TypeScript builds on JS — know JS deeply first.

resources.name: Fireship TypeScript YouTube
resources.notes: Fast 10-minute videos on TypeScript concepts. Good for initial understanding before going deep.

resources.name: Type-Level TypeScript (free book)
resources.notes: Free online book on advanced TypeScript type system. Chapter 1-6 are free.

skills.name: JavaScript (ES6+)
skills.why: JS is the foundation. If your JS fundamentals have gaps your TypeScript will have gaps too.

topics: Variables: var vs let vs const, temporal dead zone, hoisting
Functions: declarations vs expressions, arrow functions, IIFE
Scope & closures: lexical scope, closure in loops, private data with closures
this keyword: call, apply, bind — how this changes context
Prototypes: prototype chain, Object.create, class syntax as syntactic sugar
Array methods: map, filter, reduce, find, findIndex, some, every, flat, flatMap
Destructuring: arrays, objects, nested, with defaults, in function params
Spread/rest: spread arrays/objects, rest params
Template literals: interpolation, tagged templates
Promises: .then/.catch, Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, Promise.race, Promise.any
Async/await: try/catch with async, parallel with Promise.all, error propagation
Event loop: call stack, web APIs, callback queue, microtask queue — understand deeply for interviews
Modules: ES modules (import/export), CommonJS (require), interop
Generators & iterators: function*, yield, Symbol.iterator
WeakMap, WeakSet, WeakRef: memory management patterns
Proxy and Reflect: metaprogramming basics
Error handling: custom Error classes, error chaining
Performance: memory leaks, garbage collection, V8 optimizations
Web APIs: IntersectionObserver, MutationObserver, ResizeObserver
Design patterns in JS: Module, Observer, Singleton, Factory, Strategy
Functional programming: pure functions, immutability, function composition, currying

resources.name: JavaScript.info (The Modern JS Tutorial)
resources.notes: The single best free JS resource. Structured, deep, covers modern JS completely.

resources.name: You Don't Know JS (free GitHub book)
resources.notes: Kyle Simpson's free book series. 6 volumes on GitHub. Best for understanding JS deeply.

resources.name: The Odin Project JavaScript
resources.notes: Free project-based curriculum. Build real things while learning.

resources.name: javascript30.com
resources.notes: 30 free JavaScript projects in 30 days. No frameworks, pure JS.

resources.name: Event Loop Visualization
resources.notes: Free interactive event loop visualizer. Essential for understanding async JS.

skills.name: Java
skills.why: Your Java resume targets backend/ETL roles. Spring Boot is built on Java so deep Java knowledge is required.

topics: OOP fundamentals: classes, objects, constructors, access modifiers
Inheritance: extends, super keyword, method overriding, final classes
Interfaces & abstract classes: when to use each, default methods in interfaces
Collections framework: ArrayList, LinkedList, HashMap, HashSet, TreeMap, TreeSet, ArrayDeque
Generics: generic classes and methods, bounded wildcards ()
Exception handling: checked vs unchecked, try-catch-finally, try-with-resources, custom exceptions
Java 8 features: lambdas, method references, functional interfaces, Optional
Streams API: filter, map, collect, reduce, groupingBy, flatMap
Concurrency: Thread, Runnable, ExecutorService, Future, CompletableFuture
Synchronization: synchronized, volatile, atomic classes, locks
Java memory model: heap vs stack, object references, GC basics
Design patterns: Builder, Factory, Singleton, Strategy, Observer, Decorator
Java 11-17 features: var keyword, records, sealed classes, text blocks, pattern matching instanceof
JVM internals: classloader, JIT compiler, GC algorithms (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah)
Serialization: Serializable interface, Jackson ObjectMapper, JSON binding
Reactive programming: Project Reactor (Mono, Flux), RxJava basics
Java 21 virtual threads: Project Loom — massive concurrency with simple code
JVM tuning: heap sizing, GC flags, JFR profiling, JMC
Annotation processing: custom annotations, compile-time code generation
Reflection API: runtime class inspection — used heavily by Spring

resources.name: MOOC.fi Java Programming (Helsinki University)
resources.notes: Best free Java course in existence. 14 weeks, 200+ exercises, from zero to advanced.

resources.name: Baeldung
resources.notes: The most comprehensive free Java/Spring reference site. Deep articles on every topic.

resources.name: Jenkov Java Tutorials
resources.notes: Free deep tutorials on Java concurrency, NIO, reflection, design patterns.

resources.name: Java Brains (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free Spring Boot, design patterns, and Java concurrency courses.

resources.name: Dev.java (Oracle free tutorials)
resources.notes: Oracle's official free Java learning platform. Covers Java 17-21 features.

skills.name: Python
skills.why: Python is primarily useful for AI/LLM work (LangChain, FastAPI backends, data scripts). Worth keeping sharp.

topics: Syntax: indentation, variables, types, control flow, functions
Data structures: lists, dicts, sets, tuples, list/dict comprehensions
String formatting: f-strings, format(), methods
File I/O: open, read, write, with statement (context managers)
Modules & packages: import, pip, virtualenv, requirements.txt
OOP: classes, __init__, __repr__, __str__, inheritance, dataclasses
Decorators: function decorators, class decorators — used everywhere in FastAPI
Type hints: List[str], Dict[str,int], Optional, Union — mypy for checking
Error handling: try/except/finally, custom exceptions
Pydantic: data validation models — critical for FastAPI and AI backends
Virtual environments: venv, poetry, pyenv
Async Python: asyncio, async/await, aiohttp, httpx for async HTTP
FastAPI: build REST APIs with automatic OpenAPI docs — perfect for AI backends
Python with LLMs: openai library, anthropic library, langchain, litellm
Generator functions: yield, generator expressions — important for streaming LLM responses

resources.name: Python.org Official Tutorial
resources.notes: Free official Python tutorial. Thorough and always up to date.

resources.name: Real Python
resources.notes: Many free tutorials (not all — filter for free ones). Practical, project-focused.

resources.name: FastAPI Official Docs
resources.notes: FastAPI has the best documentation of any framework. Free, interactive examples.

resources.name: freeCodeCamp Python (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 4-hour full Python course for beginners.

skills.name: SQL
skills.why: SQL is tested in nearly every backend and full-stack interview. Window functions and query optimization are the most common interview gaps.

topics: SELECT: columns, aliases, DISTINCT, WHERE, comparison operators, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE
JOINs: INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN — know when each is appropriate
Aggregates: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX — with GROUP BY and HAVING
ORDER BY: ASC/DESC, multiple columns, NULLS FIRST/LAST
LIMIT and OFFSET: pagination basics
INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE: DML basics, WHERE clause safety
Subqueries: in WHERE, in FROM (derived tables), in SELECT
Window functions: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), LEAD(), LAG(), FIRST_VALUE(), LAST_VALUE() — OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...)
CTEs (Common Table Expressions): WITH name AS (...) — replace nested subqueries
Recursive CTEs: WITH RECURSIVE — for hierarchical data (org charts, file systems)
Indexes: B-tree index, when PostgreSQL uses them, composite indexes, covering indexes
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: read query execution plans, identify seq scans vs index scans
Transactions: BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT, isolation levels (READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE)
Upserts: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (PostgreSQL) — common in APIs
JSON in PostgreSQL: json vs jsonb columns, ->, ->>, #>, jsonb_set, @> containment
Query optimizer: planner statistics, ANALYZE, pg_stats, why queries go wrong
Partitioning: range, list, hash partitioning — for large tables
Materialized views: precomputed query results, REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW
Full-text search: tsvector, tsquery, GIN indexes
Row-level security: enable RLS, CREATE POLICY — used in multi-tenant SaaS
Connection pooling: PgBouncer, Supabase pooler, Neon's built-in pooling

resources.name: SQLZoo (interactive exercises)
resources.notes: Free browser-based SQL practice. Instant feedback. All levels.

resources.name: pgexercises.com
resources.notes: PostgreSQL-specific free exercises. Great for interview prep.

resources.name: Mode SQL Tutorial
resources.notes: Free structured SQL course with focus on analytics queries.

resources.name: Use The Index, Luke (free book)
resources.notes: The definitive free book on SQL performance and indexes.

resources.name: PostgreSQL Official Docs
resources.notes: Complete free reference. The window functions page alone is worth bookmarking.

resources.name: SQL Murder Mystery (fun practice)
resources.notes: Free SQL learning game. Write queries to solve a mystery. Great for beginners.

skills.name: React
skills.why: React is your primary frontend skill. Going deep here pays more than any other frontend investment.

topics: JSX: expressions, attributes, className vs class, children prop
Components: function components, props, default props, prop types
useState: primitive state, object state (spread to update), array state
useEffect: side effects, dependency array, cleanup function — the most misunderstood hook
Conditional rendering: &&, ternary, early return patterns
Lists and keys: .map() in JSX, why keys matter, key stability
Event handling: onClick, onChange, onSubmit, preventing defaults
Controlled inputs: value + onChange pattern
useReducer: complex state logic, action pattern, when to prefer over useState
useContext: create context, provide, consume — avoid prop drilling
useMemo: memoize expensive calculations — only when needed
useCallback: stable function references for child component optimization
useRef: DOM refs, mutable values without re-render, forwarding refs
Custom hooks: extract logic into reusable hooks (useFetch, useLocalStorage, useDebounce)
React.memo: prevent unnecessary re-renders of expensive components
Portals: render outside parent DOM hierarchy (modals, tooltips)
Error boundaries: catch rendering errors, fallback UI
Suspense + lazy: code splitting, lazy load components
React Router v6: BrowserRouter, Routes, Route, Link, useNavigate, useParams, useSearchParams, nested routes, outlet
React 19 new features: useOptimistic, useActionState, use() hook, server actions integration
Concurrent features: startTransition, useDeferredValue — keep UI responsive during heavy updates
Virtualization: react-window or react-virtual for large lists (1000+ items)
Compound components pattern: flexible APIs like
Render props pattern and when to prefer hooks instead
React profiler: identify unnecessary renders, expensive components
React reconciliation: how the virtual DOM diffing algorithm works

resources.name: React Official Docs (react.dev)
resources.notes: Completely rewritten in 2023. Interactive, clear, builds up from basics. The best starting point.

resources.name: Jack Herrington YouTube
resources.notes: Advanced React patterns, hooks internals, state management comparisons — all free.

resources.name: Josh W Comeau Blog
resources.notes: Deep free articles on React, CSS, animations. One of the best technical writers in frontend.

resources.name: The Odin Project React
resources.notes: Free project-based React curriculum. Build real apps, not just exercises.

resources.name: React patterns (free reference)
resources.notes: Quick reference for React patterns with examples.

resources.name: useHooks.com
resources.notes: Free collection of common custom hooks with explanations and source.

skills.name: Next.js
skills.why: Next.js is on every full-stack job description. You already use it well — go deeper on App Router internals.

topics: Pages vs App Router: understand both since many companies still use pages/
File-based routing: page.tsx, layout.tsx, loading.tsx, error.tsx, not-found.tsx
Server vs client components: the most important concept — 'use client' directive, what can be server-only
Data fetching: async server components, fetch with cache options
Image component: next/image — automatic optimization, sizes prop
Link component: next/link — prefetching behavior
Metadata API: static metadata, generateMetadata for dynamic pages
Route handlers: app/api/route.ts — GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE with NextRequest/NextResponse
Server actions: 'use server' functions, form actions, useFormState, useFormStatus
Middleware: middleware.ts at root — auth guards, redirects, A/B testing
Dynamic routes: [id], [...slug], [[...slug]] — params in server components
Parallel routes: @folder convention — modals with intercepting routes
Caching deep dive: Request Memoization, Data Cache, Full Route Cache, Router Cache — understand each separately
Revalidation: revalidatePath, revalidateTag, time-based revalidation
Auth.js v5 (you use this): session, JWT, callbacks, middleware integration
Streaming SSR: Suspense in server components, streaming HTML in chunks
Turbopack: Next.js 15 default bundler — configuration and differences from webpack
Edge runtime: middleware on edge, trade-offs vs Node.js runtime
On-demand ISR: webhooks triggering revalidation in CMS workflows
Next.js with Docker: standalone output mode, Dockerfile for Next.js
Monorepo with Next.js: Turborepo setup, shared packages

resources.name: Next.js Official Docs
resources.notes: Best source. Always current. App Router docs are the most comprehensive.

resources.name: Next.js Learn (official free course)
resources.notes: Official interactive course — builds a full dashboard app. Free.

resources.name: Lee Robinson YouTube
resources.notes: Lee works at Vercel on Next.js. Free deep dives on App Router, caching, migrations.

resources.name: Delba de Oliveira YouTube
resources.notes: Vercel engineer, free Next.js tutorials focusing on new App Router features.

resources.name: ByteGrad YouTube
resources.notes: Practical Next.js App Router tutorials, free, very clear.

skills.name: Tailwind CSS
skills.why: Tailwind is standard. Deepen knowledge of responsive design, dark mode, and custom config.

topics: Core utilities: margin, padding, width, height, display, position
Flexbox: flex, flex-row, flex-col, justify-, items-, gap-
Grid: grid, grid-cols-N, col-span-N, grid-rows-N, auto-rows
Typography: text-sm/base/lg/xl, font-normal/medium/bold, text-gray-500
Colors: text-{color}-{shade}, bg-{color}-{shade}, border-{color}-{shade}
Responsive: sm:, md:, lg:, xl:, 2xl: — mobile-first approach
Hover/focus/active: hover:, focus:, active: state modifiers
Dark mode: dark: modifier, class strategy vs media strategy in config
@apply directive: extract repeated utilities into CSS component classes
tailwind.config.js: extend colors, fonts, spacing, breakpoints, add custom values
Arbitrary values: w-[137px], text-[#123456] — escape hatch for one-off values
Group and peer modifiers: group-hover:, peer-checked: — style children based on parent/sibling state
shadcn/ui integration: understand how shadcn uses Tailwind + CSS variables + Radix UI
Animation utilities: animate-spin, animate-pulse, animate-bounce, transition-all duration-300
Tailwind v4: CSS-first config with @theme, major changes from v3
Custom plugins: plugin() API to add new utilities or components
Container queries: @container — responsive based on parent not viewport
Tailwind with Framer Motion: combining utility classes with motion animations

resources.name: Tailwind CSS Official Docs
resources.notes: Searchable, comprehensive, free. The docs have live preview for every class.

resources.name: Tailwind Labs YouTube
resources.notes: Official channel. Free screencasts building real UIs with Tailwind.

resources.name: shadcn/ui
resources.notes: Study the source code of shadcn components — best way to learn advanced Tailwind patterns.

skills.name: Node.js + Express
skills.why: Node is your primary backend runtime for full-stack roles. Deep understanding of the event loop and async patterns separates candidates.

topics: Node.js basics: require vs import, __dirname, __filename, process.env, process.exit
Built-in modules: fs (file system), path, http, os, events, stream, crypto
npm: package.json scripts, devDependencies vs dependencies, semantic versioning
Express setup: app.get/post/put/delete, req/res objects, listening on port
Middleware: app.use(), next(), middleware order matters, error handling middleware
Request parsing: express.json(), express.urlencoded(), query params vs body vs params
Environment variables: dotenv, never hardcode secrets
REST API architecture: MVC pattern, routes → controllers → services → repositories
Authentication: JWT (sign, verify, decode), bcrypt (hash, compare), refresh tokens
Error handling: centralized error handler, custom AppError class, async wrapper
Validation: Zod for request validation (you know this), express-validator alternative
Rate limiting: express-rate-limit, sliding window with Redis
CORS: same-origin policy, cors middleware config, allowed origins
File uploads: multer for multipart/form-data, storing to S3
Testing APIs: supertest for integration tests, mocking with jest
Event loop deep dive: libuv, phase diagram, microtasks vs macrotasks, nextTick
Streams: Readable, Writable, Transform, Duplex — pipe(), backpressure
Worker threads: offload CPU-intensive work, SharedArrayBuffer
Clustering: cluster module, PM2 for production process management
GraphQL with Apollo Server: schema, resolvers, DataLoader for N+1 problem
WebSockets: socket.io, real-time bidirectional communication

resources.name: Node.js Official Docs
resources.notes: Complete free reference. The 'Guides' section covers architecture deeply.

resources.name: Hussein Nasser Backend Engineering
resources.notes: The best free backend engineering channel. Covers Node.js, PostgreSQL, system design, protocols.

resources.name: The Odin Project Node/Express
resources.notes: Free project-based Node.js curriculum. Build a full app with auth.

resources.name: Node.js Best Practices (GitHub)
resources.notes: Community-maintained free guide. 100+ best practices for production Node.js.

skills.name: Spring Boot
skills.why: Your Java resume targets backend roles. Spring Boot mastery is the primary differentiator for those roles.

topics: Spring IoC container: ApplicationContext, @Bean, @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller
Dependency injection: constructor injection (preferred), @Autowired, @Qualifier
REST controller: @RestController, @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @RequestBody, @PathVariable, @RequestParam
Spring Data JPA: @Entity, @Id, @GeneratedValue, JpaRepository, custom queries with @Query
application.properties/yml: datasource, server port, profiles (dev/prod)
Spring Boot DevTools: live reload during development
Running and packaging: mvn spring-boot:run, mvn package, executable JAR
Spring Security: SecurityFilterChain, JWT filter, UserDetailsService, RBAC with @PreAuthorize
Exception handling: @ControllerAdvice, @ExceptionHandler, ProblemDetail (RFC 7807)
Spring Boot Actuator: health, metrics, info endpoints — used in production monitoring
Database migrations: Flyway or Liquibase — never change schema manually in production
Spring Batch (key for your Java resume): Job, Step, ItemReader, ItemProcessor, ItemWriter — ETL pattern
Validation: @Valid, @NotNull, @Size, custom validators with ConstraintValidator
Caching: @Cacheable, @CacheEvict with Spring Cache + Redis backend
Testing: @SpringBootTest, @WebMvcTest, MockMvc, @DataJpaTest, Testcontainers
Spring WebFlux: reactive programming with Project Reactor, Mono/Flux, R2DBC
Spring Cloud: Config Server, Eureka service discovery, Spring Cloud Gateway
Kafka with Spring: @KafkaListener, KafkaTemplate — address your resume's Kafka claim
Spring Native: GraalVM native images for fast startup
Observability: Micrometer, Prometheus metrics, structured logging with Logback

resources.name: Spring Official Guides
resources.notes: Official free guides — each guide builds a complete working feature in 15-30 minutes.

resources.name: Baeldung Spring Tutorials
resources.notes: Most comprehensive free Spring reference. Every Spring topic covered in depth.

resources.name: Amigoscode Spring Boot (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 3-hour Spring Boot full course. Builds a full REST API with PostgreSQL.

resources.name: Spring Framework Reference
resources.notes: Complete official documentation. Free.

resources.name: Spring Batch Reference Docs
resources.notes: Official Spring Batch docs — essential for your Java/ETL resume.

skills.name: Kafka (address Java resume gap)
skills.why: Kafka is listed on your Java resume but has no supporting project. This is a credibility risk that interviewers will probe.

topics: Why Kafka: event streaming vs messaging, log-based architecture, offset management
Core concepts: broker, topic, partition, offset, consumer group, producer
How messages flow: producer → broker → partition → consumer — draw this from memory
Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS: know the trade-offs for interviews
At-least-once vs exactly-once delivery: understand idempotency
Spring Kafka: @KafkaListener, @EnableKafka, KafkaTemplate, ConsumerRecord, ProducerRecord
Serialization: StringSerializer, JsonSerializer, Avro + Schema Registry
Consumer group rebalancing: what happens when a consumer joins/leaves
Offset management: auto-commit vs manual commit, earliest vs latest offset
Error handling: dead letter topics, retry with backoff, @RetryableTopic
Build something: event-driven order processing, real-time notifications, or audit log
Kafka Streams: stateful stream processing, joins, windowing
Transactions: exactly-once semantics in producer and consumer
Kafka Connect: source/sink connectors for databases
Topic partitioning strategy: partition keys, ordering guarantees

resources.name: Apache Kafka Official Docs
resources.notes: Free complete reference. Start with the 'Introduction' section.

resources.name: Confluent Kafka Tutorials (free)
resources.notes: Confluent's free interactive tutorials. Hands-on with a real Kafka cluster.

resources.name: Spring Kafka Reference Docs
resources.notes: Official Spring Kafka docs — everything you need to build with it.

resources.name: TechWorld with Nana Kafka (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 1-hour Kafka crash course. Great conceptual introduction.

skills.name: PostgreSQL (deep)
skills.why: PostgreSQL is your primary database. Interview questions go beyond basic CRUD into performance and design.

topics: Setup: install locally or use Neon (you use this), psql CLI commands
Data types: integer, bigint, text, varchar, boolean, timestamp, uuid, jsonb, array
Constraints: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, CHECK, DEFAULT
Relationships: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many with junction tables
Prisma ORM (you use this): schema.prisma, relations, migrations, Prisma Client queries
Indexes deep dive: B-tree (default), Hash, GIN (jsonb, arrays), GiST, BRIN — when to use each
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Seq Scan vs Index Scan vs Index Only Scan vs Bitmap Heap Scan
Composite indexes: column order matters — leftmost prefix rule
Partial indexes: WHERE clause in index — index only active users for example
N+1 problem: what it is, how Prisma include/select prevents it
Connection pooling: why databases max out connections, PgBouncer, Neon's pooler
VACUUM and AUTOVACUUM: dead tuples, bloat, why PostgreSQL needs this
Row-level security (RLS): ALTER TABLE ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, CREATE POLICY — for multi-tenant SaaS
pgvector extension: vector similarity search for AI/RAG — you use Neon which supports this
Partitioning: range partitioning by date, list partitioning by region
Logical replication: publication/subscription — for read replicas
Full-text search: tsvector, tsquery, to_tsvector, GIN index on tsvector column

resources.name: PostgreSQL Official Docs
resources.notes: The complete reference. Bookmark the index types page and window functions page.

resources.name: pgexercises.com
resources.notes: Free PostgreSQL exercises in your browser. Covers all SQL levels.

resources.name: Hussein Nasser PostgreSQL Playlist
resources.notes: Free deep dives on PostgreSQL internals — MVCC, indexes, EXPLAIN.

resources.name: Prisma Official Docs
resources.notes: Complete free Prisma reference.

resources.name: pgvector GitHub README
resources.notes: Free guide to adding vector search to PostgreSQL — needed for AI/RAG features.

skills.name: MongoDB
skills.why: MongoDB is used in DailyHabitz. Know it well enough to explain schema design decisions.

topics: Document model: collections, documents, BSON, _id field
CRUD: insertOne/Many, findOne/find, updateOne/Many with $set/$inc/$push, deleteOne/Many
Query operators: $eq, $ne, $gt, $lt, $in, $nin, $and, $or, $not, $exists, $regex
Mongoose: Schema, Model, virtual fields, timestamps, validation, middleware hooks
Aggregation pipeline: $match, $group, $project, $sort, $limit, $skip, $lookup (join), $unwind, $addFields
Indexes in MongoDB: createIndex, compound indexes, text indexes, TTL indexes
Schema design: embedding (1-to-few) vs referencing (1-to-many or many-to-many)
Pagination: skip/limit vs cursor-based pagination
Atlas free tier: M0 cluster, Atlas Search, connection URI with SRV
Transactions: multi-document ACID, startSession, withTransaction
Change streams: watch() for real-time data change events
Atlas Search: full-text search with fuzzy matching, autocomplete
Sharding concepts: shard key selection, range vs hash sharding

resources.name: MongoDB University (completely free)
resources.notes: Official free courses from MongoDB with certificates. Best structured learning.

resources.name: Mongoose Docs
resources.notes: Complete free Mongoose reference.

resources.name: MongoDB Manual
resources.notes: Complete official MongoDB reference.

skills.name: Redis
skills.why: Redis appears in a huge percentage of backend job descriptions. Rate limiting, caching, and session management are all common interview topics.

topics: What Redis is: in-memory data store, not a primary DB, persistence options
String commands: SET key value EX seconds, GET key, DEL key, EXISTS key, INCR key
Key expiry: EXPIRE, TTL, PERSIST — how Redis handles time-to-live
List commands: LPUSH, RPUSH, LPOP, RPOP, LRANGE — use as queue or stack
Hash commands: HSET, HGET, HGETALL, HDEL — like a nested object
Set commands: SADD, SMEMBERS, SISMEMBER, SUNION, SINTER
Sorted set commands: ZADD, ZRANGE, ZRANK, ZSCORE — leaderboards, rate limiting
Upstash setup: create free serverless Redis, get connection URL, use @upstash/redis in Next.js
Cache-aside pattern: check Redis → if miss, query DB → store in Redis → return
Rate limiting with Redis: INCR + EXPIRE pattern, sliding window with sorted sets
Session storage: store JWT sessions, token blocklist for logout
Pub/Sub: SUBSCRIBE channel, PUBLISH channel message — real-time messaging
MULTI/EXEC transactions: atomic operations
Connection management: ioredis vs @upstash/redis — when to use each
Cache invalidation: the hard problem — strategies: TTL, write-through, event-driven
Redis Streams: XADD, XREAD, XREADGROUP — persistent message queue (Kafka-lite)
Lua scripting: EVAL for atomic multi-step operations
Redis Cluster: horizontal sharding, hash slots
Redis persistence: RDB snapshots vs AOF append-only file
Redis with BullMQ: job queue library on top of Redis — background job processing

resources.name: Redis University (free courses + free certification)
resources.notes: All courses AND certification are completely free. Best structured Redis learning.

resources.name: Redis Official Docs with Interactive Tutorials
resources.notes: Documentation includes interactive sandbox to run Redis commands in browser.

resources.name: Upstash Docs
resources.notes: Serverless Redis that works with Vercel/Next.js. Free tier is generous.

resources.name: BullMQ Docs
resources.notes: Job queue library on Redis. Free. Useful for background jobs in Node.js.

cert.name: Redis Certified Developer — completely free exam
cert.desc: Complete RU101 on Redis University (free), then take the certification attempt (also free). Adds real credibility to your backend resume. Do this after adding Redis to one project.

skills.name: AWS
skills.why: You already deploy on AWS. Go deeper on the services you use and understand enough to speak to architecture decisions in interviews.

topics: IAM: users, groups, roles, policies — principle of least privilege, never use root
EC2: instance types, AMIs, key pairs, security groups (stateful firewall), elastic IPs
S3: buckets, objects, storage classes, presigned URLs, bucket policies, CORS
RDS: managed PostgreSQL, parameter groups, automated backups, read replicas, Multi-AZ
VPC basics: subnets (public vs private), route tables, internet gateway, NAT gateway
CloudFront: distributions, origins, cache behaviors, invalidations, Lambda@Edge
Cognito: user pools vs identity pools, hosted UI, JWT tokens, app clients
Lambda: function creation, triggers (API Gateway, S3, SQS), cold starts, memory vs duration
API Gateway: REST API vs HTTP API, integration with Lambda, CORS configuration
SQS and SNS: queue vs topic, fan-out pattern, dead letter queues
ECS Fargate: running Docker containers without managing EC2 servers
CloudWatch: logs, metrics, alarms, log groups, insights queries
CDK (Cloud Development Kit): define AWS infra with TypeScript — better than clicking console
Well-Architected Framework: 6 pillars — operational excellence, security, reliability, performance, cost, sustainability
Cost optimization: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, Spot instances, right-sizing
Multi-region: Route53 health checks, failover routing, data residency considerations

resources.name: AWS Free Tier (use it)
resources.notes: Always on free tier: EC2 t2.micro, S3 5GB, RDS db.t2.micro 20GB, Lambda 1M requests/mo.

resources.name: freeCodeCamp AWS Cloud Practitioner (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 14-hour comprehensive AWS course covering all exam domains.

resources.name: AWS Skill Builder (free courses)
resources.notes: Official AWS training platform. Many courses are free with just an email.

resources.name: Cloud Quest (free game on Skill Builder)
resources.notes: Free AWS learning game. Build real cloud solutions in a simulated environment.

cert.name: AWS Cloud Practitioner (CLF-C02) — $100, highest ROI cert for your resume
cert.desc: Study for free with freeCodeCamp YouTube + AWS Skill Builder. Take ~20 practice exams on Examtopics.com (free). Schedule when scoring 80%+ consistently. This cert appears on most job descriptions as 'nice to have' and signals real AWS commitment beyond just using it for projects.

skills.name: Docker
skills.why: Docker is on nearly every job description and is the gateway to understanding containers, microservices, and Kubernetes. Not having it is an easy filter.

topics: Why Docker: 'works on my machine' problem, containers vs VMs, image vs container
Docker architecture: Docker daemon, client, registry (Docker Hub), images, containers
Your first Dockerfile: FROM (base image), WORKDIR, COPY, RUN (build time), EXPOSE, CMD/ENTRYPOINT (run time)
Core commands: docker build -t name . | docker run -p 3000:3000 name | docker ps | docker stop | docker rm | docker logs
Port mapping: -p hostPort:containerPort — why this is needed
Environment variables: --env-file .env or -e KEY=value
Volumes: -v hostPath:containerPath — persist data, dev hot reload
Docker Hub: docker pull, docker push, public vs private registries
Multi-stage builds: use node:alpine to build, then copy only dist/ to smaller final image — reduces image size 10x
Docker Compose: define multiple services (app + postgres + redis) in docker-compose.yml
Compose commands: docker compose up -d, down, logs, exec, ps
Networking in Compose: service names as hostnames (your app talks to 'postgres' not 'localhost')
Health checks: HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile, depends_on with condition in Compose
.dockerignore: exclude node_modules, .env, .git from build context
Non-root user: security best practice — don't run containers as root
Docker in GitHub Actions: build and push image as part of CI pipeline
Docker with Next.js: standalone output mode (output: 'standalone' in next.config.js) for minimal images
Docker with Spring Boot: layered JARs, Jib plugin (no Dockerfile needed)
Container scanning: docker scout, trivy — vulnerability scanning images
Buildx: multi-platform builds (amd64 + arm64)
Kubernetes concepts from Docker: pods = containers, deployments, services, ingress

resources.name: Docker Official Docs + Get Started Tutorial
resources.notes: Official free tutorial. Walks from installation to multi-container Compose apps.

resources.name: TechWorld with Nana — Docker Full Course (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 3-hour course. Most recommended Docker intro on YouTube. Very clear.

resources.name: Play with Docker (free online lab)
resources.notes: Free browser-based Docker environment. No installation needed. 4-hour sessions.

resources.name: freeCodeCamp Docker + Kubernetes (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free comprehensive course covering Docker through Kubernetes.

skills.name: GitHub Actions / CI-CD
skills.why: You already use GitHub Actions. Go deeper to be able to design complete pipelines in interviews.

topics: Workflow YAML structure: name, on, jobs, steps — understand every key
Triggers: push, pull_request, workflow_dispatch, schedule (cron)
Runners: ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest, self-hosted
Steps: uses (actions), run (shell commands), with (inputs), env
Common actions: actions/checkout@v4, actions/setup-node@v4, actions/setup-java@v4
Secrets: Settings → Secrets, reference as ${{ secrets.SECRET_NAME }}
Job dependencies: needs: [job1, job2] — run jobs sequentially or in parallel
Matrix builds: test on Node 18, 20, 22 simultaneously
Caching: actions/cache for node_modules, .m2 Maven cache — speeds up pipelines
Artifacts: actions/upload-artifact, download-artifact — pass files between jobs
Environments: staging, production environments with required reviewers
Deploy to Vercel from Actions: vercel CLI in workflow or use Vercel GitHub integration
Deploy to AWS from Actions: configure-aws-credentials action with OIDC (no long-lived keys)
Docker in Actions: build image, push to ECR or Docker Hub
Reusable workflows: workflow_call trigger, pass inputs — avoid duplication across repos
Composite actions: create your own custom actions in JavaScript or shell
OIDC authentication: passwordless AWS access from GitHub Actions — no AWS keys in secrets
Semantic release: automate versioning and changelog from commit messages

resources.name: GitHub Actions Official Docs
resources.notes: Complete free reference. The 'Understanding GitHub Actions' page is essential.

resources.name: TechWorld with Nana CI/CD (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 2-hour GitHub Actions crash course.

skills.name: Terraform
skills.why: IaC knowledge shows you understand production infrastructure. Appears on many backend/DevOps-adjacent JDs.

topics: Why IaC: reproducible infrastructure, version controlled, no manual clicking
HCL syntax: resource blocks, variable blocks, output blocks, locals
Providers: hashicorp/aws provider, provider configuration, authentication
Core commands: terraform init, terraform plan, terraform apply, terraform destroy
State file: terraform.tfstate — what it contains, why you should not edit it manually
First resource: write Terraform to create an S3 bucket and EC2 instance
Variables: variable blocks, tfvars files, sensitive = true, validation rules
Outputs: output blocks — expose resource attributes for use elsewhere
Modules: reusable infrastructure components, module sources, input/output
Remote state: S3 backend + DynamoDB locking — never use local state in production
Data sources: look up existing AWS resources not managed by this Terraform
Reproduce your AWS project: write Terraform for your EC2 + RDS + S3 + CloudFront setup
Workspaces: dev/staging/prod — separate state per environment
Terraform Cloud free tier: remote state, variable sets, remote runs
Import: import existing manually-created resources into Terraform state
Testing: terraform validate, tflint, terratest for integration testing

resources.name: HashiCorp Learn — Terraform (free official tutorials)
resources.notes: Official free tutorials. Structured paths for AWS, Azure, GCP.

resources.name: freeCodeCamp Terraform + AWS (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 2.5-hour Terraform course focused on AWS.

resources.name: Terraform Official Docs
resources.notes: Complete free reference for all Terraform features.

cert.name: HashiCorp Terraform Associate (003) — $70 exam, optional
cert.desc: Study for free with HashiCorp Learn tutorials. Good for backend/DevOps-adjacent roles. Lower priority than AWS cert. Take it once you've written real Terraform for your projects.

skills.name: LLM API Integration
skills.why: AI integration is the #1 differentiator in the 2026 job market. A single shipped AI feature puts you ahead of 80% of candidates.

topics: How LLMs work conceptually: next-token prediction, context window, temperature, top_p
OpenAI API basics: /v1/chat/completions endpoint, model names, messages array (system/user/assistant roles)
Anthropic Claude API: same concepts, messages format, system prompt as separate field
API keys: environment variables, never expose in frontend code, use server-side proxy
Basic request: model, max_tokens, messages — handle response and display
Error handling: rate limit errors (429), context length errors, timeout handling
Streaming responses: stream: true — display tokens as they arrive in Next.js with ReadableStream
Vercel AI SDK: useChat hook, streamText, generateText — cleanest way to add AI to Next.js
System prompts: define AI persona, constrain outputs, inject context
Structured outputs: JSON mode, response_format: {type: 'json_object'}, Zod parsing
Function calling / Tool use: define tools the LLM can call, parse tool_use responses
Token counting and cost: understand pricing, estimate costs before production
Model selection: GPT-4o vs GPT-4o-mini vs Claude Sonnet vs Haiku — cost/quality trade-off
Context window management: truncate history, sliding window, summary compression
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): embed documents, store in vector DB, retrieve relevant chunks, inject into prompt
Agentic systems: LLM that calls tools in a loop to complete a task
LangChain/LangGraph: orchestration framework for multi-step LLM workflows
Evaluations: automated testing of LLM outputs, reference-based vs model-based evals
Fine-tuning concepts: when it's needed (rare), how it differs from RAG
MCP (Model Context Protocol): emerging standard for LLM tool integration

resources.name: Vercel AI SDK Docs
resources.notes: Best way to integrate AI into Next.js. Free docs, clean abstractions.

resources.name: DeepLearning.AI Free Short Courses
resources.notes: Free short courses with Andrew Ng. Each is 1-2 hours.

resources.name: OpenAI API Docs
resources.notes: Complete API reference. Free to read, small credit to experiment.

resources.name: Anthropic API Docs
resources.notes: Claude API documentation — well written, excellent tool use docs.

resources.name: OpenAI Cookbook (GitHub, free)
resources.notes: Free collection of code examples for every OpenAI API use case.

skills.name: RAG Systems
skills.why: RAG is how you make LLMs useful with your own data. It's the most practical AI engineering skill companies need.

topics: Why RAG: LLMs have a knowledge cutoff, can hallucinate — RAG grounds them in real data
The RAG pipeline: Load documents → Chunk → Embed → Store in vector DB → Query → Retrieve → Augment prompt → Generate
Embeddings: text → vector of numbers that captures meaning, similar text = similar vectors
OpenAI embeddings API: text-embedding-3-small model, 1536 dimensions
Cosine similarity: how vector databases find the most similar chunks
Simple RAG in JavaScript: embed docs, store in array, find closest match, inject into prompt
Vector databases: pgvector (PostgreSQL extension — free, works with your Neon DB), Pinecone free tier
Chunking strategies: fixed size (500 tokens), recursive character splitting, semantic chunking
Metadata filtering: filter by source, date, user_id before vector search
Hybrid search: combine keyword search (BM25) + vector search for better recall
Retrieval evaluation: are we retrieving the right chunks? precision and recall
LangChain RAG: DocumentLoader, TextSplitter, Embeddings, VectorStore, RetrievalQA chain
Advanced retrieval: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings), multi-query retrieval
Re-ranking: use a cross-encoder to re-score retrieved chunks
Self-querying retriever: LLM generates the vector DB filter from natural language
Production RAG: caching embeddings, async embedding pipelines, monitoring retrieval quality

resources.name: DeepLearning.AI — Building RAG with LangChain (free)
resources.notes: Free 1-hour course. Best structured intro to RAG.

resources.name: pgvector GitHub + Docs
resources.notes: Add vector search to your existing Neon PostgreSQL. Free.

resources.name: LangChain JS Docs
resources.notes: LangChain for JavaScript/TypeScript — use in your Next.js projects.

skills.name: Jest + React Testing Library
skills.why: Testing is asked in virtually every technical interview. Being able to write good tests signals production maturity.

topics: Test structure: describe() for grouping, it()/test() for cases, beforeEach/afterEach for setup/teardown
Matchers: toBe, toEqual (deep), toBeTruthy, toBeFalsy, toBeNull, toContain, toHaveLength, toThrow
Async tests: async/await in tests, resolves/rejects matchers
React Testing Library: render(), screen.getByRole(), screen.getByText(), screen.getByTestId()
Queries: getBy (throws if not found), queryBy (returns null), findBy (async wait)
User interactions: userEvent.click(), userEvent.type(), userEvent.clear()
What to test: user behavior, not implementation — don't test internal state
Mocking: jest.fn() for functions, jest.mock('module') for modules, jest.spyOn(obj, 'method')
Mock implementations: mockReturnValue, mockResolvedValue, mockRejectedValue
MSW (Mock Service Worker): intercept API calls at network level — better than mocking fetch
Testing hooks: renderHook() from React Testing Library
Testing with context: wrap render() in custom wrapper with providers
waitFor: wait for async updates, assertions inside waitFor
Coverage: jest --coverage, understand what to aim for vs what's waste
Integration tests: test entire feature flows, realistic data
Testing Zustand/Redux: test store actions and selectors independently
Snapshot testing: when it helps (stable UI) vs when it hurts (brittle noise)
Performance testing: React profiler in tests

resources.name: Jest Official Docs
resources.notes: Complete free reference. The 'Expect' API page is your most-used reference.

resources.name: Testing Library Docs + Guiding Principles
resources.notes: Read the Guiding Principles page first — it changes how you think about testing.

resources.name: Kent C. Dodds Testing Blog Posts (free)
resources.notes: The creator of React Testing Library. Free blog posts on testing philosophy and practice.

resources.name: MSW (Mock Service Worker) Docs
resources.notes: The modern way to mock APIs in tests. Free.

skills.name: Playwright
skills.why: Playwright is in your Ghumakkad project. Add it to your skills section now — it's a real differentiator since most candidates only know unit testing.

topics: Setup: npx playwright install, playwright.config.ts, test runner
Core commands: page.goto(url), page.click(selector), page.fill(selector, value), page.waitForURL()
Locators (modern API): page.getByRole(), page.getByText(), page.getByLabel(), page.getByTestId()
Assertions: expect(page).toHaveURL(), expect(locator).toBeVisible(), toHaveText(), toHaveValue()
Test structure: test(), test.describe(), test.beforeEach() — same as Jest
Running tests: npx playwright test, --ui mode (interactive), --headed (see browser)
Page Object Model: encapsulate page selectors and actions in a class — maintainable tests
API testing: request.get/post — test your API routes directly in Playwright
Network interception: page.route(url, handler) — mock API responses in E2E tests
Screenshots + videos: on failure, full page screenshots, video recording
Multi-browser: chromium, firefox, webkit — run same tests across all three
Authentication state: storageState to reuse logged-in sessions across tests
Playwright in GitHub Actions: install browsers, run tests, upload report as artifact
Visual regression: expect(page).toHaveScreenshot() — pixel-by-pixel comparison
Accessibility testing: @axe-core/playwright — automated a11y audit in E2E tests
Sharding: split test suite across multiple CI runners for speed

resources.name: Playwright Official Docs
resources.notes: Best Playwright resource. Includes codegen tool that writes tests by recording your clicks.

resources.name: Playwright YouTube Channel
resources.notes: Official free tutorial videos from the Playwright team.

skills.name: Data Structures & Algorithms
skills.why: DSA is tested in every technical interview. A strong resume gets you the interview — DSA knowledge gets you the offer.

topics: Arrays — Two Pointers: sorted array, palindrome check, 3sum, remove duplicates
Arrays — Sliding Window: max subarray sum, longest substring without repeat, minimum window substring
Hash Maps: two sum, group anagrams, top K frequent, valid anagram, contains duplicate
Stacks: valid parentheses, min stack, daily temperatures, largest rectangle in histogram
Binary Search: classic sorted array search, search in rotated array, find minimum in rotated
Linked Lists: reverse linked list, merge two sorted lists, detect cycle (Floyd's), find middle
Trees — DFS: inorder/preorder/postorder, max depth, same tree, path sum, validate BST
Trees — BFS: level order traversal, minimum depth, connect level pointers
Heap / Priority Queue: kth largest element, top K frequent, merge K sorted lists, find median from stream
Graphs — BFS: number of islands, rotting oranges, shortest path in binary matrix, clone graph
Graphs — DFS: course schedule (topological sort), number of connected components, Pacific Atlantic water flow
Dynamic Programming: climbing stairs, house robber, coin change, longest common subsequence, 0/1 knapsack, longest increasing subsequence
Backtracking: subsets, permutations, combination sum, letter combinations of phone number
Tries: implement trie, word search II, longest word in dictionary
Union-Find (Disjoint Set): number of connected components, redundant connection
Monotonic Stack: next greater element, trapping rain water
Intervals: merge intervals, insert interval, meeting rooms
Bit manipulation: single number, number of 1 bits, reverse bits, missing number

resources.name: NeetCode.io — NeetCode 150 (free)
resources.notes: The best curated free list. 150 problems organized by pattern. Covers 90% of what appears in interviews.

resources.name: NeetCode YouTube (free video solutions)
resources.notes: Free video solution for every NeetCode problem. Clear explanations with drawings.

resources.name: LeetCode (free tier)
resources.notes: ~2000 free problems. Use NeetCode list to choose which ones. Premium not required.

resources.name: Visualgo.net
resources.notes: Free visual animations of sorting algorithms, graph traversal, data structures.

resources.name: Big-O Cheat Sheet
resources.notes: Free quick reference for time and space complexity of common algorithms and data structures.

resources.name: Blind 75 (free list)
resources.notes: Classic curated 75 problems. Subset of NeetCode 150 — start here if time is tight.

skills.name: System Design for Interviews
skills.why: System design rounds are now standard even at mid-level SWE roles. Being able to talk through architecture decisions signals seniority.

topics: Client-server model: how a request goes from browser to server and back, every step
DNS: domain → IP resolution, A records, CNAME, TTL
HTTP/HTTPS: request/response, headers, methods, status codes, HTTP/2 vs HTTP/1.1
TCP vs UDP: reliability vs speed, when each is used (TCP=HTTP, UDP=video streaming)
IP addressing: IPv4, CIDR notation, public vs private IPs
Load balancers: L4 vs L7, algorithms (round robin, least connections, IP hash), health checks
Horizontal vs vertical scaling: add more servers vs bigger server, trade-offs
CAP theorem: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance — pick 2 in distributed systems
Caching layers: browser cache, CDN, application cache (Redis), database cache — know when each applies
Database scaling: read replicas for read-heavy loads, sharding for write-heavy, when to denormalize
Indexes in system design context: trade-off: faster reads, slower writes, more storage
Message queues: Kafka/SQS — async decoupling, event-driven, fan-out, dead letter queues
Rate limiting: token bucket algorithm, sliding window — protect your APIs
Content Delivery Network (CDN): static assets at edge, CloudFront, cache-control headers
Consistent hashing: distribute load across nodes without reshuffling everything
SQL vs NoSQL decision: structured/relational = SQL, flexible schema/scale = NoSQL
URL shortener: hashing, redirects, analytics, custom slugs — very common interview
Rate limiter: token bucket in Redis, per-user vs global limits
Notification system: push, email, SMS — fan-out, delivery guarantees, deduplication
Job application tracker (ApplyVibe): YOU KNOW THIS — scale to 1M users: read replicas, Redis cache, CDN, queue for AI processing
Chat application: WebSockets, message ordering, read receipts, push notifications
File storage service (like S3): chunked upload, deduplication, CDN distribution

resources.name: System Design Primer (GitHub — free)
resources.notes: Best free system design resource. 255k+ GitHub stars. Covers every fundamental.

resources.name: ByteByteGo Blog (free tier)
resources.notes: Alex Xu's system design newsletter. Many articles free. Visual explanations.

resources.name: Gaurav Sen System Design (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free system design deep dives. Excellent for building intuition.

resources.name: Hussein Nasser (YouTube)
resources.notes: Backend engineering fundamentals that underpin all system design.

resources.name: hello-interview.com System Design (free)
resources.notes: Free structured system design guide aimed at SWE interviews specifically.

resources.name: Excalidraw (free diagramming tool)
resources.notes: Free, browser-based whiteboard for drawing system diagrams. Use this to practice.

skills.name: AWS Cloud Practitioner (CLF-C02)
skills.why: Most impactful cert for your profile. Shows AWS commitment beyond just deploying a project. Appears on many JDs as 'nice to have' or required.

topics: Domain 1 — Cloud Concepts (24%): what is cloud, benefits of AWS, IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, total cost of ownership, cloud economics
Domain 2 — Security (30%): shared responsibility model, IAM, MFA, encryption, compliance (HIPAA, GDPR), Shield, WAF, Inspector

Domain 3 — Technology (34%): compute (EC2, Lambda, ECS), storage (S3, EBS, EFS, Glacier), database (RDS, DynamoDB, ElastiCache), networking (VPC, CloudFront, Route53), developer tools (CodePipeline, CloudFormation)
Domain 4 — Billing (12%): pricing models, Free Tier, cost explorer, budgets, support plans, organizations
Week 1: Watch freeCodeCamp YouTube course (14 hours — do 2hrs/day)
Week 2: AWS Skill Builder official free modules, take notes
Week 3: Practice exams — Examtopics.com (free), target 80%+ score
Week 4: Review weak areas, final practice, schedule and take exam
Question format: multiple choice and multiple response — eliminate wrong answers first
No coding: this is conceptual — you're tested on when to use which service
Compute: EC2 (virtual servers), Lambda (serverless), ECS (containers), Elastic Beanstalk (PaaS)
Storage: S3 (object), EBS (block, attached to EC2), EFS (shared file), Glacier (archive)
Database: RDS (managed relational), DynamoDB (NoSQL), ElastiCache (Redis/Memcached), Redshift (data warehouse)
Networking: VPC, Subnets, Security Groups, NACLs, Route53 (DNS), CloudFront (CDN), Direct Connect
Security: IAM (identity), KMS (key management), Secrets Manager, Shield (DDoS), WAF
Management: CloudWatch (monitoring), CloudTrail (audit), CloudFormation (IaC), Trusted Advisor

resources.name: freeCodeCamp AWS Practitioner Full Course (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 14-hour comprehensive video course. All you need for the exam.

resources.name: AWS Cloud Practitioner Essentials (Skill Builder)
resources.notes: Official free AWS course on Skill Builder. Structured modules.

resources.name: Examtopics CLF-C02 Practice Questions (free)
resources.notes: Free practice exam questions with community explanations.

resources.name: AWS Whitepapers (free)
resources.notes: Official AWS whitepapers — Well-Architected Framework and Overview of AWS are exam-relevant.

cert.name: Cost: $100 | Study time: 3-4 weeks | Pass score: 700/1000 | Validity: 3 years
cert.desc: Book the exam at Pearson VUE testing center or online proctored. Study until you score 80%+ on practice tests consistently. This is the one certification that most frequently appears in recruiter conversations and job descriptions.

skills.name: Redis Certified Developer
skills.why: Completely free — courses and exam. Quick win that adds backend credibility.

topics: RU101 — Introduction to Redis Data Structures: strings, lists, hashes, sets, sorted sets — with hands-on labs
RU102JS — Redis for JavaScript developers: use node-redis and ioredis — most relevant for you
RU102 — Redis Streams: persistent message streaming — XADD, XREAD, consumer groups
RU203 — Querying, Indexing, Full-Text Search: RediSearch module
Format: 60 questions, 90 minutes, online proctored or at home
Topics: data structures, streams, persistence, replication, clustering, security
Passing score: 67%
Cost: FREE — unlimited attempts
Timeline: 1-2 weeks after finishing RU101 and RU102JS

resources.name: Redis University (all courses + exam free)
resources.notes: Enroll in RU101 first. All courses have interactive exercises and real Redis clusters to use.

cert.name: Cost: FREE (courses + exam) | Timeline: 1-2 weeks | Priority: medium
cert.desc: Do this immediately after adding Redis to a project. It's free, quick, and adds genuine credibility to your backend resume. One of the only truly free industry certifications.

skills.name: HashiCorp Terraform Associate (003)
skills.why: Shows IaC knowledge. Good for backend and DevOps-adjacent roles.

topics: Understand IaC concepts: benefits, use cases, Terraform's place in the IaC landscape
Terraform purpose: what problems it solves, key features
Terraform basics: providers, resources, state, variables, outputs, modules
Use the Terraform CLI: init, plan, apply, destroy, workspace, output, state, fmt, validate
Interact with Terraform modules: use public modules, create modules, input/output
Navigate the core workflow: write → plan → apply cycle
Implement and maintain state: remote state, state locking, state commands
Read, generate, and modify configuration: data sources, functions, count/for_each
Understand Terraform Cloud: workspaces, variable sets, remote runs
Week 1: HashiCorp Learn AWS path — do all tutorials hands-on with real AWS
Week 2: Write Terraform for your own AWS project (EC2 + RDS + S3)
Week 3: Take practice exams, review weak areas
Cost: $70, Pearson VUE

resources.name: HashiCorp Learn — Terraform AWS Path (free)
resources.notes: Official free tutorials that directly cover exam content.

resources.name: freeCodeCamp Terraform Course (YouTube)
resources.notes: Free 2.5-hour Terraform + AWS course.

cert.name: Cost: $70 | Study time: 2-3 weeks | Validity: 2 years | Priority: optional
cert.desc: Take after AWS cert and Terraform practice on real projects. Lower priority than AWS cert and Redis cert but good for roles that list Terraform in the JD.
